text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Whitaker, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH102938 NHS No. 9876543210  
12 Brookfield Road, London, SE15 3AB

Thank you for referring this patient for review. I saw him today in the neurology clinic, accompanied by his partner. He has engaged with previous education on aura recognition strategies and was able to describe his warning features clearly in his own words. He reports a brief sensation of “time slipping sideways,” a dull pressure behind the eyes, and a metallic taste, typically lasting 10–20 seconds before awareness is affected. He states that his events have been relatively infrequent over the past three years and summarises the overall pattern as absence seizures twice a year. He notes that these brief episodes are sometimes followed by 5–10 minutes of fatigue and mild confusion but no prolonged disorientation. He denies nocturnal events to his knowledge and there is no clear history of convulsive activity from witnesses.

He has used a written checklist and a phone note to capture triggers and early warning signs. He identifies poor sleep and skipped meals as the most consistent antecedents. He has practised pause-breath-focus techniques learned from prior counselling sessions and asks family members to avoid rapid questioning if he signals that a warning is starting. He feels this approach has reduced escalation of symptoms on several occasions. There have been no injuries, tongue-biting, or urinary incontinence with recent episodes. Driving is currently not undertaken by the patient.

On examination today he was alert, oriented, and cooperative. Neurological examination was unremarkable, including cranial nerves, limb power, tone, reflexes, coordination, and gait. There were no focal neurological deficits. Review of his prior investigations shows a routine EEG from 2023 reported as non-diagnostic with no interictal epileptiform discharges, and a brain MRI from the same year reported as structurally normal. Blood tests available from his GP in May 2025, including FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, and B12, were within reference ranges.

We discussed ongoing self-management with emphasis on consolidating his aura recognition strategies. He has developed a personalised action plan: at first warning he sits, breathes slowly, and minimises sensory load; his partner is aware to provide space and observe quietly. He keeps a brief diary noting context, sleep, hydration, and stress levels to help refine patterns. He understands when to seek urgent care if the pattern changes, if episodes cluster, or if prolonged confusion occurs.

Given the reported stability and the low frequency of events—specifically, absence seizures twice a year by his account—and in the absence of red flags, we agreed to continue monitoring with safety advice and lifestyle optimisation. He is comfortable with this plan and prefers to avoid additional tests for now. Should the history evolve, we can consider extended EEG monitoring or repeat imaging. He will contact us sooner if frequency increases, if he develops new features (e.g., prolonged loss of awareness, injuries, nocturnal events witnessed), or if his daily functioning is impacted.

I have provided written information reinforcing his recognition and response steps and advised continued use of a simple seizure diary. I have also encouraged regular sleep patterns, hydration, and consistent meals. He is aware of first aid guidance and has shared this with close contacts. We will arrange a routine follow-up in 9–12 months, with an open appointment should there be any change.

Thank you for your helpful referral. Please let me know if there is any additional information that would assist.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: absence seizures twice a year by his account

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient's current event rate: 'absence seizures twice a year by his account.' This is reiterated as 'the low frequency of events—specifically, absence seizures twice a year.' 'Twice a year' translates to 2 per year per formatting rules. No other higher frequency is mentioned, and there are no clusters or multiple types with different rates to reconcile.
seizure_frequency_number: absence seizures twice a year by his account
